title: Government Employees and the AIADMK Government
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, factual detail, and paragraph order. Names, dates, figures, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. Where Tamil idiom cannot be reproduced literally without sounding unnatural, the English follows its sense and rhetorical force without adding claims absent from the source. The Tamil below is a source-checked derived reading copy in which clear OCR character, punctuation, name, and word-break errors have been repaired. The canonical source file remains unchanged and authoritative. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement.
paragraphs: Whether they are government employees or teachers, the AIADMK rulers' “allergy” to them did not begin only after returning to power this time. I need not remind you how harshly they behaved when Jayalalithaa was Chief Minister from 1991 to 1996 and from 2001 to 2006.

The AIADMK government enacted a new law solely to suppress peaceful, democratic protests by public servants. As though this were Hitler's time, it provided up to three years' imprisonment, a fine of Rs 5,000, or both, for those who struck in breach of it and those who encouraged them. Jayalalithaa once told the Assembly that government employees and teachers protesting must return to work by 31 October 2002, failing which ESMA and every other available law would be unleashed upon them; they would be removed in stages and replaced. She spoke as though employees and teachers were enemies upon whom war was being prepared.

Government employees went on strike from 13 April 2003. The AIADMK government cancelled the recognition of every participating union. Police entered union leaders' homes at night, arrested them under ESMA and imprisoned them. In a single order, 170,000 striking employees and teachers were dismissed wholesale. Newspapers wrote that no Chief Minister in Indian political history had issued such a cruel order. These events proved that authoritarian rule was performing its savage dance in Tamil Nadu.
If public servants suffered continually under earlier AIADMK governments, suicides and attempted suicides among them have now become a continuing story under this regime.

Unable to conceal the matter despite all their efforts, the AIADMK rulers arrested a Minister as a scapegoat. Officer Muthukumarasamy jumped before a train and died, leaving his family helpless. What cruelty! To what degree must the rulers have tormented him mentally for him to take that decision? In what unlawful ways are they trying to divert the investigation? Newspapers report one person and another being questioned, but why do investigators conceal what they said? Whom are the police struggling to protect? They removed a Minister. Was he collecting funds only for himself? What is the full meaning of his interview saying, “Amma knows everything”?

Was Minister “Agri” alone engaged in compulsory collections? Are there not several Ministers in this Cabinet who behave with still greater arrogance? The facts may not yet have emerged. Not only ordinary employees, but IAS and IPS officers too are subjected to pressure. A few may cooperate because they gain something themselves. But how far are honest officers chased toward the margins of administration? A weekly magazine wrote: “They may not have killed themselves like Muthukumarasamy, but every minute they die and come back to life.”

It was reported that Agriculture Department officer Senthil gave a statement saying the Minister ordered him to pressure and threaten Muthukumarasamy into collecting money, and that he therefore threatened the officer. Is that true? If former Minister Agri were likewise to give a truthful statement, who else would be caught?

When a person is arrested in such a criminal case, his home and office are ordinarily searched. Why was no such search conducted after Agri Krishnamurthy's arrest? Police custody is ordinarily sought immediately, but not here. When he was produced before the magistrate, a copy of the statement taken during the preliminary inquiry would ordinarily be filed. That too does not appear to have happened. When reporters asked him about the case, he said, “Amma knows everything.” The CB-CID investigators do not appear to have taken notice. Taken together, the steps so far look like an eyewash staged to deceive the opposition. Agri Krishnamurthy may perhaps be rescued, but no one can conceal the fact that people throughout Tamil Nadu speak of him and this government as responsible for Muthukumarasamy's death.

Did public-servant suicides end with Muthukumarasamy? A few days ago, government employee Shanmugavel died by hanging in the nutritious-meal division office within the Ramanathapuram Collectorate. What are the facts of that case?
At Tiruchirappalli Government General Hospital, RMO Nehru attempted suicide, reportedly because of harassment from higher authorities. That too occurred under this AIADMK government.
Thiagarajan, Tahsildar at Ambasamudram in Tirunelveli district, and Rajendran, an engineer in Tirunelveli Corporation, died by suicide under this government.
Ration-shop employee Ilango died by suicide in Chennai's Thangasala area. Reports said threats from officials drove him to it. What are the details?

And now, near Tiruvarur, my own constituency, a government employee who set himself on fire because he could not bear an officer's “torture” has died in hospital. What answer will this government give his family? Muthukrishnan, thirty-eight, of Ammaiyappan village, worked as an overseer in the Nannilam Panchayat Union office. He set himself on fire on the evening of the third and died early on the fourth. Before dying he gave a dying declaration to the police and to Judicial Magistrate Kavitha. On that basis, police registered a case against the Executive Engineer.

In Coimbatore, Sakthivel and his sister Vasantha worked in ration shops. A loan dispute arose between Vasantha and the AIADMK councillor of Corporation Ward 62, and she complained to police. When the councillor allegedly threatened her to withdraw it, Sakthivel swallowed sixteen sleeping tablets in an attempt to die. He is receiving treatment. In a handwritten letter, he identified the AIADMK councillor as the cause.
Earlier, Palanisamy, who worked for the Housing Board in Erode, died by suicide, reportedly unable to bear harassment by this government.

And before all these, about fifteen of the 13,000 People's Welfare Workers dismissed merely because they had been appointed under the DMK government died by suicide under this AIADMK regime. Thus suicide among public servants has continued as a tragic occurrence.

Last month, Puthiya Thalaimurai weekly itself published a long article headed: “Conscientious officers struggle with mental stress; rulers victimise and toss officers about.” When Perarignar Anna took office in 1967, he said party members must not directly approach officials to get things done. Had today's rulers read that, they would not have behaved so harshly as to drive several officers toward suicide.

If rulers decide to make a few weak officials partners in their corruption and conduct collective plunder, governance itself becomes something one must search for and discover. Today's rulers have no time to worry about these matters, or even think about them for a moment. Udanpirappē, do you not know what their sole concern is?
With affection,
M.K.
